title: About
text: Hello, My name is Logan Whitten and I am a sophomore majoring in Sports Media and Communications, as well as broadcasting. I hope with this blog to dive deep into some of the more overthought questions in sports using stats and data to back up my points

title: Looking Back at the Historically Bad 2022 Quarterback Draft Class
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(ggrepel)




Code
draft <- read_csv("draft.csv") 


The 2022 NFL draft class is heading into the end of their rookie contracts. After 4 years, only one quarterback has remained on their original team. This quarterback also happened to be the last overall pick in the entire draft as the 49ers got an absolute steal in Brock Purdy.Now what about all the quarterbacks drafted before him? Well, lets look at the game log of everybody here


Code
draftqbs <- draft |> 

  filter(Pos == "QB") 




Code
qbgames <- draftqbs |> 

  select(Player, St, G)




Code
qbgames |> gt() |> cols_label( St = "Years as a Starter", G = "Total Games Played" ) |> tab_header( title = "2022 NFL Quarterback Draft Class Game Log" ) |> tab_style( style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold",), locations = cells_title("title") ) |> tab_source_note( source_note = md("By: Logan Whitten | Source: profootballreference 2022 NFL Draft class") ) 








2022 NFL Quarterback Draft Class Game Log


Player
Years as a Starter
Total Games Played




Kenny Pickett
2
36


Desmond Ridder
1
25


Malik Willis
0
22


Matt Corral
0
0


Bailey Zappe
0
15


Sam Howell
1
20


Chris Oladokun
0
4


Skylar Thompson
0
10


Brock Purdy
2
49



By: Logan Whitten | Source: profootballreference 2022 NFL Draft class








As we can see here, the only two quarterbacks to have more than two seasons of being the primary starter are the first and last quarterbacks selected, Kenny Pickett and Brock Purdy. Behind them is the trio that is most interesting to me of Desmond Ridder, Malik Willis, and Sam Howell, who were all once thought of to be the future of the teams that drafted them and were given the playing time to prove that, but ultimately flamed out in the end.
Now why is this? If this class was given a fair shot to prove their worth, what went wrong? For that we turn to the good ole touchdown to interception ratio.


Code
stackeddraftqbs <- draftqbs |> 

  select(Player, PassingTD, PassingInt) |> 

  pivot_longer( 

    cols=-Player, 

    names_to="Stat", 

    values_to="Total" 

  ) |> 

  mutate( 

    Total = case_when( 

      is.na(Total) == TRUE ~ 0, 

      TRUE ~ Total 

    ) 

  ) 




Code
ggplot() + 

  geom_bar(data = stackeddraftqbs, aes(x=reorder(Player, Total), weight = Total, fill = Stat)) + 

  scale_fill_manual(values=c("red", "darkgreen"), name="Passing Stats", labels = c("Interceptions", "Touchdown passes")) +  

  coord_flip() +  

  labs( 

    title = "2022 quarterback draft class career stats", 

    x="", 

    y="Touchdown passes vs interceptions", 

    caption = "Source: Pro Football reference | By: Logan Whitten" 

  ) + 

  theme_minimal() +  

  theme( 

    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold") ) 









Here we can see that Purdy obviously tops everyone else but it seems that it is a tighter race for that second place spot between Sam Howell and Kenny Pickett then one might hope for a first vs a fifth rounder. The latter pick is actually ahead of the first qb taken in this scenario, despite throwing more interceptions than touchdowns in his career.Pickett and Ridder are also neck and neck in terms of touchdown passes, with 4th round career backup Bailey Zappe, and Malik Willis lagging behind. It is important to note here that Matt Corral was also a high pick in the third round, but never played a snap in the NFL
Now I know what you’re thinking, stats don’t tell the whole story. This is true to an extent. While strictly using box score stats isn’t the truly best way to measure a player’s true value, Pro Football Reference has found a way where you can best estimate what a player’s value is with the wAV, or weighted approximate value statistic. Approximate value is a method to attempt to describe the value of a player’s season in a single number, while wAV takes the approximate value of each season and sums them all up in weighted format, which is 100% of the player’s best season, 95% of their second best season and so on. Let’s take a look here at the 2022 quarterback class’ wAVs compared to where they were drafted


Code
qbgrades <- draftqbs |> 

  mutate( 

    value = wAV, 

    games = G 

  ) 




Code
ggplot() +  

  geom_point( 

    data=qbgrades,  

    aes(x=value, y=games, size= Rnd), 

     color = "red") + 

  geom_text_repel( 

    data=qbgrades,  

    aes(x=value, y=games, label=Player) 

  ) +  

  scale_size_continuous(range = c(5,1)) + 

  annotate("rect", fill = "lightgreen", alpha = .3, 

           xmin = 20, xmax = Inf, 

           ymin = 34, ymax = Inf) + 

  geom_text(aes(x=30, y=45, label="Good value")) + 

  labs( 

    title = "Overall value of 2022 quarterback draft class", 

    x = "total value", 

    y = "games played", 

    caption = "Source: Pro Football reference | By: Logan Whitten" 

  ) + 

  theme_minimal() +  

  theme( 

    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold") 

  ) 









Once again, Purdy is head and shoulders above the rest, but we already know that. Let’s look at the rest of this class. We see here that the graph has a wAV above 20 is considered to be a good value player and we also see that zero of this class outside of Brock even reaches that score, with the closest being first-rounder Kenny Pickett with a score of just under 15. It’s even more bizarre that 2 of the 3 third rounders have a score under 10 for their entire careers!
It’s safe to say that we found out a few things today. One obviously being that Brock Purdy is by far the best out of this quarterback class and it’s not even close. But more importantly, if he wasn’t here, this class would easily be among the worst of all time based on contribution, stats and actual true player value.
